caveats: a: OEWS evidence is at NAICS 3221, not six-digit 322120, and is not a direct AI-exposure measure.
a: The frozen labor ratio is inherited from ancestor NAICS 3221 rather than observed for paper mills alone.
rho: No representative five-year AI adoption study for US paper mills was found.
rho: The estimate excludes ordinary controls and capital automation unless AI directly enables the exposed task reduction.
e: The frozen dataset declares the LMM firm count missing; this eligibility share cannot repair or substitute for that absent denominator.
e: Repeated commodity sales are not automatically recurring outsourced services.
s5: Neither proxy represents paper mills or the target EBITDA band.
s5: The frozen n input is missing and must remain missing; no availability total can be inferred.
q: Public sources describe grade trends, not the pricing terms of eligible private mills.
q: Fiber, energy, chemical, freight, tariff, and capacity changes can swamp AI savings in realized prices.
d5: The G.17 series covers NAICS 32212 and is not isolated to the lens population.
d5: Capacity and production do not directly equal constant-price, constant-quality customer demand.
o: This is an inference from process and regulation rather than an observed five-year operator share.
o: Consolidation can eliminate a current lens operator without eliminating the physical production function.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Qualifying mills earn repeated per-ton, per-roll, contract, or tolling revenue under fixed, indexed, market-linked, or cost-plus supply arrangements.
lens.deviation_from_default: Paper mills principally sell manufactured intermediate products, and many are integrated or commodity-oriented. The lens is narrowed to independent mills with recurring outsourced-production relationships.
lens.excluded_activities: Integrated captive production, spot-only commodity sales, one-off transactions, paperboard mills, converted-product manufacturing, shells, non-control vehicles, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market paper mills only where converters, distributors, brands, institutions, or industrial customers repeatedly outsource specialty, tissue, packaging-paper, technical-paper, private-label, or contract production.

a.bridge: BLS reports that other production occupations represent 42.60% of employment across pulp, paper, and paperboard mills, and EPA describes multiple physical papermaking and wastewater-control subcategories. I translate that embodied, continuous-process occupation mix into limited AI substitution, concentrated in planning, maintenance analysis, quality and laboratory documentation, purchasing, compliance, sales administration, and customer service. Broader 3221 employment may overstate pulp and paperboard roles and understate information work in specialty paper mills.
caveats: OEWS evidence is at NAICS 3221, not six-digit 322120, and is not a direct AI-exposure measure.
The frozen labor ratio is inherited from ancestor NAICS 3221 rather than observed for paper mills alone.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted NAICS 322120 occupation table plus task-time studies at integrated and purchased-pulp mills across printing-writing, tissue, packaging, and technical grades.
a.rationale: Machine tending, process control, maintenance, utilities, material handling, testing, safety, and effluent treatment dominate. AI can reduce information-processing work and avoid some indirect hiring but cannot replace most continuous physical operation.

d5.bridge: The Federal Reserve's paper-mill industrial-production index was 56.2066 in May 2026 on a 2017=100 basis. AF&PA reports 2024 printing-writing capacity down 6.9%, tissue capacity down 1.7%, and packaging-paper capacity up 4.6%. I use these observed but broader output and capacity measures to build a grade-mix-weighted declining base with a wide range. They measure aggregate production/capacity, not constant-quality demand for qualifying LMM outsourced mills, and packaging paper may be stronger than printing-writing.
caveats: The G.17 series covers NAICS 32212 and is not isolated to the lens population.
Capacity and production do not directly equal constant-price, constant-quality customer demand.
d5.change_evidence: AF&PA or Fastmarkets five-year tonnage forecasts by paper grade, reconciled with G.17 production, imports, exports, announced machine closures/conversions, and the eligible firms' actual customer mix.
d5.rationale: Digital substitution continues to erode printing-writing and some newsprint demand. Tissue and packaging-paper uses are more durable, while specialty and technical grades vary; conversions and closures can tighten surviving niches even as total paper volume falls.

caveats: The frozen dataset declares the LMM firm count missing; this eligibility share cannot repair or substitute for that absent denominator.
Repeated commodity sales are not automatically recurring outsourced services.
e.change_evidence: A facility census linking ownership, mill grade, integrated status, customer contracts, recurring revenue, and EBITDA-band eligibility.
e.rationale: Most output is commodity product sold by integrated or large mills, which does not meet the service lens. A minority of independent specialty, private-label, purchased-pulp, and tolling mills may repeatedly perform outsourced production for external customers.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) at ancestor 3221: labor compensation (L02) $10,436.5M / sectoral output (T30) $73,870.4M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $752,091 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 32212); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

o.bridge: EPA identifies physical processes spanning purchased-pulp fine and lightweight papers, tissue, filter and non-woven papers, secondary-fiber grades, and regulated effluent treatment. I translate those material processes and permit accountabilities into a near-total requirement for a mill operator for surviving quantity. This may overstate persistence of today's independent firm type if converters integrate or production consolidates.
caveats: This is an inference from process and regulation rather than an observed five-year operator share.
Consolidation can eliminate a current lens operator without eliminating the physical production function.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy research, converter vertical-integration plans, autonomous-mill evidence, machine consolidation, and regulatory changes affecting operating responsibility.
o.rationale: AI cannot form, dry, finish, test, and ship paper or hold the mill's environmental and product accountability. Staffing can fall and expertise can centralize, but nearly all surviving quantity still requires an operating mill organization.

caveats: Public sources describe grade trends, not the pricing terms of eligible private mills.
Fiber, energy, chemical, freight, tariff, and capacity changes can swamp AI savings in realized prices.
q.change_evidence: Contract samples and quote-to-margin histories by grade, including index formulas, fiber and energy surcharges, rebid cadence, minimum volumes, qualification duration, and customer concentration.
q.rationale: Index-linked and negotiated per-ton pricing, excess capacity, grade substitution, and sophisticated converters pass much of a productivity gain to customers. Specialty qualification, private-label switching costs, service reliability, and fixed-price contract periods can preserve a portion.

caveats: No representative five-year AI adoption study for US paper mills was found.
The estimate excludes ordinary controls and capital automation unless AI directly enables the exposed task reduction.
rho.change_evidence: Audited multi-mill deployments reporting sustained avoided hours and quality, downtime, energy, and compliance outcomes for AI-enabled systems.
rho.rationale: Predictive-maintenance triage, grade-change planning, energy and quality advisory, procurement, work orders, compliance reporting, and commercial administration are feasible within five years. Legacy control systems, cybersecurity, sparse failure data, uptime requirements, customer qualification, and environmental permits constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year ownership-transfer intentions for US employer businesses and BizBuySell records small-manufacturing sales. I use them only as a prior, discounted for paper mills' specialized assets, environmental history, energy and fiber dependencies, strategic ownership, consolidation, and potential closure rather than transfer. Marketplace transactions skew much smaller and less regulated, so they likely overstate liquidity.
caveats: Neither proxy represents paper mills or the target EBITDA band.
The frozen n input is missing and must remain missing; no availability total can be inferred.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year facility-level deal, divestiture, closure, conversion, and restart census from Fastmarkets, PitchBook, permits, and corporate filings, paired with owner succession interviews.
s5.rationale: Specialty or nonintegrated mills may transfer through owner succession or corporate divestiture, but site-specific permits, machine condition, customer qualifications, working capital, and closure economics shrink the buyer pool. A missing LMM firm count prevents translating this probability into expected deal count.

principal_driver: Complex continuous operations create valuable AI-assisted maintenance, quality, energy, and compliance decisions.
principal_weakness: The eligible LMM firm universe is unmeasured, while commodity pricing and shrinking graphic-paper demand limit durable benefit.
